notes.title: Writing sound instead of spelling
notes.description: How Fonora began: a writing system that encodes where and how a sound is produced — a composable grid of place and manner — instead of inheriting an alphabet.
notes.abstract: The founding question — can a script encode articulation (place x manner + a vowel marker) rather than orthography?

notes.title: Teaching the machine to hear
notes.description: Turning arbitrary text into Fonora symbols with a phonetic pipeline: Text -> eSpeak NG -> IPA -> normalize -> encode -> decode, and the source-of-truth debt it created.
notes.abstract: A phonetic pipeline (eSpeak -> IPA -> normalize -> encode) beats spelling rules — but leaves a split source of truth.

notes.title: How few vowels can English tolerate?
notes.description: The v2 double-vowel experiment: how a compact vowel inventory that merged lexical sets within a key produced 100% round-trips but high-impact homographs like bed/bird.
notes.abstract: A compact 13-key vowel inventory merged lexical sets within keys — round-trips passed, but bed/bird collided.

notes.title: Vowels as grammar: the v3 rebuild
notes.description: Replacing merged double-vowels with a fixed symbol grammar (simple vowel = two symbols, diphthong = four) that resolves the worst v2 homographs without exploding the symbol count.
notes.abstract: Vowels become a fixed symbol grammar instead of merged keys, splitting MOUTH from GOAT and retiring the v2 marker.

notes.title: Hunting ambiguity in the script
notes.description: An exhaustive audit of where Fonora symbol composition produces collisions and greedy-decoder hazards — and the design decisions it surfaced but did not make.
notes.abstract: Exhaustive inventory + concatenation analysis: zero exact collisions, but real homograph hazards await design calls.

notes.title: One script for every language
notes.description: Encoding many languages from a shared IPA pipeline, and the Spanish "perro" vowel bug that proved English vowel rules must be scoped per language.
notes.abstract: A shared pipeline plus an English-only vowel overlay — discovered by fixing a Spanish vowel bug.

notes.title: Can words grow from a grid? (Gen 1 and Gen 2)
notes.description: The first two generations of Fonoran vocabulary: hand roots with grammar-vowel inflection, then coordinate-driven roots with Indo-European collision repair.
notes.abstract: Gen 1 grew word families from grammar-vowels; Gen 2 added collision repair — both stayed concept-first and English-adjacent.

notes.title: Meaning from coordinates: the Gen 3 DDA experiment
notes.description: Gen 3 inverted the problem: meaning as a position in <Depth, Mode, Aspect>, with familiar concepts like "river" appearing only as derivations, never primitives.
notes.abstract: Gen 3 treated meaning as a coordinate in the articulation grid — river = flow + path = "xaeli".

notes.title: Making invented words memorable (Gen 3.1)
notes.description: Gen 3.1 improved learnability while leaving DDA coordinates unchanged, spreading phonetic vowels within native pools to raise memorability from 70 to 85.
notes.abstract: A distinctiveness layer over Gen 3: memorability 70 -> 85 and 100% parseable, at a 31% grid-repair cost.

notes.title: Optimal sounds, wrong premise
notes.description: A Huffman-like allocation gave ~200 ranked human primitives optimal syllables — and revealed that optimizing sound before agreeing on meaning was the wrong order.
notes.abstract: 200 primitives got phonetically optimal syllables, but grammar leaked into roots and compounds were flat and unteachable.

notes.title: The irreducible dimensions of meaning
notes.description: Asking what ~100 dimensions of reality (not English words) let compounds write themselves, using the "fundamental experience test" and deferring phonetics entirely.
notes.abstract: Meaning first: ~99 primitive dimensions chosen by a fundamental-experience test, with sounds deliberately deferred.

notes.title: Concepts are canonical, sounds are editorial proposals
notes.description: The converged CV/CVC pipeline: priority-driven sound assignment with collision and boundary scoring as aids, human review as the gate, and approved roots locked through every rebuild.
notes.abstract: A converged concept -> root -> compound -> lab pipeline where scoring advises and humans decide; all 118 roots approved.

notes.title: Compiling English into meaning
notes.description: An English -> Fonoran compiler that resolves to the nearest approved concepts rather than word-for-word glosses, with a resolution cascade and honest red tokens for gaps.
notes.abstract: A three-layer compiler resolves English to the nearest approved concepts and shows unresolved words honestly in red.

notes.title: Grammar as particles, not words
notes.description: Deciding which relationships — tense, logic, deixis, pronouns — belong in a closed grammatical class rather than the root lexicon, and reaching 100% translation coverage.
notes.abstract: Tense, logic, and deixis become closed-class particles, not roots — moving causation out of the lexicon.

notes.title: The campfire test: communication over correctness
notes.description: The pivot that reframed everything: Fonoran as a cross-lingual communication experiment where success is recoverable meaning, judged by the campfire test and human playtests.
notes.abstract: Fonoran is an experiment in recoverable meaning, not a perfect ontology — generators propose, humans approve.

notes.title: Typing an invented script
notes.description: Closing the read-write loop with a visual QWERTY IME, keyboard testing, and spelling practice so learners can physically produce Fonora symbols.
notes.abstract: A visual QWERTY IME plus spelling and typing practice let learners produce — not just read — the script.

notes.title: Can strangers recover meaning?
notes.description: The current frontier: a puzzle-conversation protocol and playtests that measure recoverable meaning between two root-knowers better than any automated score.
notes.abstract: The live edge of the notebook — testing whether two root-knowers can recover each other’s intended meaning.

notes.title: Reconstructing compounds under the constitution
notes.description: Phase IV kickoff: rebuild the compound inventory as teaching trees and meaning-attempts under the constitution.
notes.abstract: The Phase IV frontier — reconstructing compounds as communicative strategies, not canonical answers.
